name: My door sticks and leaks air—do I need a full replacement or just a repair?
headline: My door sticks and leaks air—do I need a full replacement or just a repair?

mainEntity.name: My door sticks and leaks air—do I need a full replacement or just a repair?
mainEntity.text: My door sticks and leaks air—do I need a full replacement or just a repair?

acceptedAnswer.text: If your door is sticking, drafty, or hard to latch, it’s not always necessary to replace the entire unit. Sometimes a repair is enough. For example, minor sticking can often be fixed by tightening or adjusting hinges, planing a small area of the door edge, or adjusting the strike plate on the frame. Drafts around an otherwise solid door may be solved with new weatherstripping, a better door sweep, or tightening the threshold. If the door itself is in good shape but the frame has minor gaps, careful caulking and insulation can help. However, if you see serious problems like rot at the bottom of the door or frame, warping that won’t correct with hinge adjustments, or visible cracks, replacement might be smarter. Old metal doors with rust and loose skins are also candidates for replacement. Pitfalls include doing cosmetic fixes while ignoring deeper issues. For example, surface rot on a coastal home may hide more serious damage behind trim. Painting over problems won’t fix them. Also, adding heavy weatherstripping without correcting alignment can make the door even harder to close. A simple way to decide is to look at age, damage, and how many parts seem wrong. If you’re constantly patching and the door still feels insecure, replacement may cost more up front but save effort and stress over time.
